title: Make a pinch pot
subtitle: The no-equipment starter: one ball of clay, two hands
excerpt: The starter pottery project that needs no wheel, no kiln, and no tools beyond the hands. A single ball of clay opened with the thumb, walls pinched outward between thumb and fingers, a sponge to smooth the rim. Default path uses air-dry clay (no kiln needed); an earthenware path is noted for readers with kiln access.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (c. 1885), § Hand-Modelling in Clay: the thumb-press opening and the rotational pinch sequence are documented unchanged. The two-tracks framing (air-dry path / earthenware-with-kiln path) follows the pottery-pipeline scope split rather than a specific source. The pinch-pot technique itself is centuries older than any cited reference; Bernard Leach in A Potter's Book (1940) notes it as the first technique taught in every working pottery.

content.text: The
pinch pot

is the starter form every clay tradition teaches first. One ball of clay; the thumb opens a central well; the pinch between thumb and fingers walks the walls outward and upward into a small bowl shape. The whole project takes thirty minutes to an hour from picking up the clay to a finished pinched form ready to dry. Air-dry clay is the default path here, no kiln, no equipment beyond the hands and a sponge. An earthenware path is noted at the end for readers with kiln access who want a fired ceramic result.
The pinch pot teaches every hand-building idea that comes next. The clay gives under even pressure. The rim cracks first if not
compressed

. A wall thinned past a certain point falls in. A form dried too fast cracks at the base. The pinch pot is the first technique taught in almost every working pottery. It is older than the wheel by several thousand years.

attrs.body: Earthenware path only: wet-clean the workspace after each session; never sweep dry clay residue.
attrs.title: Earthenware path note

content.text: What you need
A 400-500 g ball of clay

. Default path: air-dry clay (DAS, Crayola, or any unfired air-dry brand). Earthenware path: red or white earthenware from a clay supplier, wedged before use. A ball this size makes a finished pot around 8 cm tall and 10 cm across.
A bowl of water
for dampening fingers and sponging the rim. Cold tap water is fine.
A small sponge
. Natural sea sponge if you have one; a cut square of kitchen sponge serves as a stand-in.
Optional: wooden rib, needle tool, wire cutter

. The pot can be made with hands only; the rib smooths the outer wall once the form has set up, the needle tool levels the rim, the wire cutter trims a clean ball off a larger block of clay. Toothpicks, kitchen sponges, and unwaxed dental floss work as stand-ins.
Method
1. Roll the ball

Cut a 400-500 g piece of clay from the bag. Roll it between both palms into a smooth, even ball with no surface cracks. The ball should hold its round shape under the weight of one palm without sagging; if it sags, the clay is too soft and needs a few minutes uncovered to firm slightly. If it cracks under rolling pressure, the clay is too dry; wrap in a damp cloth for ten minutes before continuing. The ball is the foundation; an uneven ball produces an uneven pot.
2. Open the well

Hold the ball in the cup of your non-dominant hand. Press the thumb of your dominant hand straight down into the centre of the ball, stopping when the thumb-tip is about 1.5 cm short of the bottom (don't break through). The thumb opens a central well; the bottom of the pot will be the wall between the thumb-tip and the cup of the holding hand.

Check the bottom thickness. A finger pressed against the outside should feel the thumb-tip from the inside through a layer about 1-1.5 cm thick. Too thin and the bottom collapses; too thick and the pot reads as squat and heavy. Adjust by pressing the thumb a fraction further in if the bottom feels too thick.
3. Pinch the walls, first round

Keep the thumb of the dominant hand inside the well; the index and middle finger of the same hand support the outside of the wall. Pinch firmly but evenly, the clay moves between the thumb and the fingers. As the pinch lands, rotate the pot a small amount (about 30°) in the holding hand. Pinch, rotate, pinch, rotate; one full revolution of the pot is the first round of pinching.

Start the first round at the very base of the well (where the wall meets the bottom) and work upward to the rim in a spiral motion. The clay moves outward and upward with each pinch; the wall thins by about 2-3 mm per round. After one full round, the pot's wall is roughly 1 cm thick and the rim is about 1 cm higher than where it started.
4. Pinch the walls, subsequent rounds

Repeat the spiral pinch from base to rim. The wall thins by 1-2 mm per round in the later rounds. Aim for a finished wall thickness of 6-8 mm, thinner than a beginner expects, but thick enough not to collapse. Three or four rounds of pinching usually does it. Pause between rounds to check the wall thickness with thumb-and-finger across the rim; correct any thick patches by adding an extra pinch where they sit.

If the clay starts to feel stiff and unresponsive (it stops moving under the pinch), the surface has dried slightly. Dampen the wall with a sponge or a wet finger to recover. If the wall feels too soft and starts to sag under its own weight (the rim drooping in any direction), the clay is too wet, set the pot upright on the work surface, leave for ten minutes, and continue once the clay has firmed slightly.
5. Level and compress the rim

When the wall thickness is even and the pot has the rough shape you want, level the rim. Hold the pot rim-up. Look across the top from eye level: any high or low patches show as wobbles against the horizon. Pinch the high patches down or push the low patches up gently with a fingertip until the rim runs level all the way around.

Once level, compress the rim. The rim is the most-stressed part of any pot and will crack first if uncompressed. Pinch each section of the rim once more between thumb and forefinger, pressing inward to compact the clay. If the rim looks wavy or rough, slide a slightly damp sponge over it in a single pass, once around the rim, no scrubbing. A heavy pass with the sponge softens the clay too much; one light pass smooths.
6. Settle and refine

Set the pot upright on the work surface. Step back and look at it from across the table. Where does the wall sit unevenly? Where does the rim wobble? Where is the surface bumpy? Address each from soft-clay state, pinch a thick spot, smooth a bump with a damp finger, level the rim with another light sponge pass. Don't add or remove much clay at this stage; the form is set and over-correction shows as compression marks.
For an air-dry path: the pot is finished. Leave on the work surface for 24-48 hours to dry slowly, fast drying cracks the clay. Once
bone-dry

(cool-to-the-touch test: a fully dry pot feels room-temperature, a damp one feels noticeably cool), the air-dry pot is display-ready. Decorate with acrylic paint and seal with a brush-on acrylic varnish for an indoor display piece.
Earthenware path (kiln-required)

If the pot was made in earthenware with a view to kiln-firing, the drying is the same, 24-48 hours on the work surface, slow and even, until bone-dry. The pot then bisque-fires to cone 06 (around 999°C) for about 8 hours including ramp-up. Bisque-fired pinch pots take a brush-on or dipped glaze; cone-06 commercial low-fire glazes are forgiving on first attempts. A second glaze firing to cone 06 finishes the pot. Both firings happen in the same kiln, in the same temperature range.

For the earthenware path, treat clay dust with normal pottery care. Wet-clean the workspace. Wear a P100 respirator when handling glaze. Do not share the air with kitchen surfaces. The wedging tutorial covers preparing the clay before pinching. The glazing tutorial covers the bisque-to-glaze steps.
Variations
Joined pinch-pot sphere
. Two pinch pots of the same size, scored at the rims and joined to make a hollow sphere. The starting point for sculpture, beads, and rattle pots.
Pinch pot with a coil rim

. Add a coil at the rim of the pinch pot to extend its height. Score the rim, attach a rolled coil with slip, smooth the join, and continue pinching. The coil-extended pinch pot is the transition between pure pinch and coil-built pottery.
Thumbprint texture
. Don't smooth the outer wall with the sponge; leave the finger-pinches visible as texture. The pot reads as obviously handmade, which is the point of the form.
Care

Air-dry pinch pots are display-only, not water-tight, not food-safe, not dishwasher-safe. Use them for keys at the door, change in the kitchen, or a single dried flower. Earthenware pots that have been bisque-fired and glazed are food-safe for dry storage; check the glaze label for water-tight rating before serving wet food. Wash earthenware pots by hand with mild detergent; the rim is the wear point and benefits from a soft cloth rather than an abrasive sponge.
If the pot went sideways

attrs.intro: Five things that go wrong on a first pinch pot and the fix for each.

items.fix: If the crack is shallow, wet the rim slightly and pinch the crack closed; smooth with a damp finger. If deep, the pot may need to be re-formed before it dries further. Next pot: compress the rim deliberately as the last step before drying.
items.cause: Rim was not compressed before drying; the loose clay particles at the rim shrink unevenly as moisture leaves.
items.symptom: Pot cracks at the rim while drying or in the first hour

items.fix: Press the thin patch from the outside with a wet finger to add tiny amounts of clay back; let the pot rest 10 minutes; check again. A pot with one thin patch is structurally weaker than its overall thickness suggests. Next pot: rotate the pot more often during pinching to even out the pressure.
items.cause: Pinching was uneven, some sections got more pressure than others.
items.symptom: Wall has thin patches that show light through when held up to a window

items.fix: If the crack is shallow, smooth wet clay into it and let dry slowly under cover. Next pot: check the base thickness at the opening stage (step 2), 1-1.5 cm is the target; thicker bases crack more often.
items.cause: Base was too thick relative to the wall; the base shrinks more than the wall and stresses the join.
items.symptom: Bottom of the pot cracks during drying

items.fix: Set the pot upright on the work surface and leave 10-15 minutes to firm slightly. Continue pinching with lighter pressure. If the slump is severe, re-ball the clay and start over; the structural integrity is lost.
items.cause: Clay too soft to support its own weight; or pot was pinched too thin too fast.
items.symptom: Pot sags or slumps during pinching, the wall droops outward

items.fix: Once the wall thickness is right, slide a damp finger (or a wooden rib, or a damp sponge in a single pass) over the outer surface to smooth the marks. Don't over-work; one or two passes is enough. Heavy sponging dilutes the surface and weakens the wall.
items.cause: Each pinch left a finger mark and the marks weren't smoothed.
items.symptom: Surface texture too rough, wall looks bumpy and uneven

attrs.heading: Common first-pinch-pot problems
